Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: The right-side containers are toggle switches. Clicking red toggles the red bar between horizontal (rows 30-33, cols 12-17) and vertical (rows 24-29, cols 18-21). Clicking blue toggles blue objects between (solid square + scattered dots) and (checkerboard square + solid block). All 4 toggle combinations have been visited without completing the level. The goal must involve more than just toggling containers.
- Goal model: Need to find additional interactions. Maybe clicking on left-side objects, moving the green center, or clicking on the yellow square does something.
- Plan: Try clicking on the yellow square or other left-side objects to discover new mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 of about 59 target actions so far.

Row 20: c c c c b c b g g Y Y g g c c c

So in the substring (cols 15-30), position 0 is col 15, position 1 is col 16, etc.

Actually, the green center is at cols 20-21, which is near the right edge of the left side. Let me try moving it right to see if it can cross the divider.

Let me re-check. The green center was at rows 30-31, cols 18-19 before the action. After the action, it's at rows 30-31, cols 20-21. So it moved right by 2 columns (from 18-19 to 20-21), but didn't move up at all.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the red container while green center is in dark red box
# Red container is at rows 17-21, cols 45-54 (approximately)
# Let me find the exact position

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'R' and n['pixels'] > 40:
        print("Red container: boundary=%s, pixels=%d" % (n['boundary'], n['pixels']))
        # Calculate center
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        center_row = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
        center_col = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
        print("  Center: row=%d, col=%d" % (center_row, center_col))

# Try clicking on the red container
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 48}])
print("\nAfter clicking red container:")
print("  board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
print("  level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))

# Check green center position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print("Green center: boundary=%s" % n['boundary'])

# Check if any objects changed
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print("\nArea (rows 28-36, cols 5-25):")
for i in range(28, 37):
    if i < len(lines):
        row_str = lines[i][5:26] if len(lines[i]) > 25 else lines[i][5:]
        print("  %d: %s" % (i, row_str))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
